evidence: src/shared/lib/errorHandling/handleError.ts combines normalization/logging with browser event-bus wiring and singleton notifier lifecycle (lines 25-70), while broad callers in hooks/features depend on it directly (e.g., src/shared/hooks/useMobile.tsx line 2, src/shared/components/MediaGallery/hooks/useMediaGalleryHandlers.ts line 3).

src/shared/components/ui/tooltip.tsx exports both low-level UI primitives and a high-level touch orchestration component with stateful pointer/document listeners (lines 51-124), and page/feature components consume the same module for different abstraction levels (e.g., src/pages/Home/components/CreativePartnerPane.tsx line 2, src/shared/components/MediaGalleryItem/components/InfoTooltip.tsx lines 3-7).

Media gallery behavior is split across orchestration wrappers and large context bags: src/shared/components/MediaGallery/hooks/useMediaGalleryController.ts forwards grouped state/actions to another hook rather than owning a clear boundary (lines 13-84), and src/shared/components/MediaGallery/hooks/useMediaGalleryHandlers.ts mixes shot navigation, modal control, context syncing, and error handling in one seam (lines 16-146).

Modal/dialog control contracts are inconsistent across sibling components (`open/onOpenChange` vs `isOpen/onClose`), reducing predictability of ownership boundaries: src/shared/components/ConfirmDialog.tsx lines 35-46, src/shared/components/LoraSelectorModal/components/DescriptionModal.tsx lines 6-14, src/pages/Home/components/InstallInstructionsModal.tsx lines 7-43.

Task payload construction is repeated as large hand-built param bags across multiple task modules (e.g., join clips `buildJoinClipsPayload`, individual travel segment `buildOrchestratorDetails` + `buildTaskParamsPayload`, travel payload builder `buildTravelBetweenImagesPayload`, magic edit `buildMagicEditTaskParams`).

Edit/lightbox code has multi-layer wrapper chains that mostly remap data (e.g., `VideoLightboxContent` -> `useVideoLightboxRenderModel` -> `LightboxLayout`, plus separate `useButtonGroupProps` builders repeating shared fields).
Inline edit state/value has a dual-shape adapter path (`UseImageEditValueLegacyParams` and `toModelsFromLegacy`) plus parallel model assembly in `useInlineEditState`, increasing indirection cost.
Three timestamp hooks are thin wrappers over `useLiveRelativeTimestamp` with minor naming differences, indicating micro-fragmentation instead of one focused formatter-based API.

Several async flows swallow failures without centralized reporting or propagation (e.g., `useAdjacentPagePreloader` has empty catch blocks at lines 159-161 and 191; `StrokeOverlay` ignores pointer-capture errors at lines 166 and 231).

Related task/generation utilities use different propagation contracts for similar failure types: `preloadGenerationTaskMappings` catches and suppresses errors after `handleError` (generationTaskBridge.ts:142-144), while task creators in `imageGeneration.ts`/`magicEdit.ts`/`joinClips.ts` call `handleError` and rethrow (e.g., imageGeneration.ts:428-430, 513-514).

User-action hooks also diverge: `useDiscordSignIn` handles returned auth errors via `handleError` but does not throw/return failure state (useDiscordSignIn.ts:26-31), while mutation hooks generally throw from mutationFn and surface through `onError` (useGenerationMutations.ts:48-50, 98-103, 136-142, 159-161).

findings.summary: Shared error utility acts as both domain error pipeline and UI event bus, creating a mixed-responsibility architectural hub.
evidence: handleError module owns notifier registration, window listener lifecycle, and CustomEvent dispatch (src/shared/lib/errorHandling/handleError.ts lines 25-70) in addition to normalization/logging (lines 78-94).

Feature hooks import this mixed module directly for non-UI concerns, coupling them to a browser-aware shared hub (src/shared/hooks/useMobile.tsx line 2; src/shared/components/MediaGallery/hooks/useMediaGalleryHandlers.ts line 3).
findings.suggestion: Split into layered modules: keep pure error normalization/logging in one core module, move notification transport to a UI adapter module, and have UI entrypoints compose them so non-UI hooks depend only on the core API.

findings.summary: A single tooltip module mixes primitive wrappers with product-specific touch behavior, collapsing abstraction layers.
evidence: Tooltip module defines primitive exports and also `TouchableTooltip` with local open state and global pointerdown listener logic (src/shared/components/ui/tooltip.tsx lines 51-124).

Different consumers import from the same module for basic primitives and behavior-heavy usage patterns, which broadens shared UI surface ownership (src/pages/Home/components/CreativePartnerPane.tsx line 2; src/shared/components/MediaGalleryItem/components/InfoTooltip.tsx lines 3-7).
findings.suggestion: Keep `ui/tooltip` limited to primitive wrappers; move touch-aware orchestration into feature-level components/hooks (for example `shared/components/tooltipBehaviors/`) so global UI primitives stay narrowly scoped.

findings.summary: Media gallery controller/handler seam relies on large nested context bags, obscuring ownership boundaries and increasing ripple risk.
evidence: Controller accepts three broad context objects and mostly repackages them to downstream hooks (src/shared/components/MediaGallery/hooks/useMediaGalleryController.ts lines 13-84).

Handlers module combines multiple concerns (shot navigation, modal transitions, context-driven side effects, error reporting) in one hook surface (src/shared/components/MediaGallery/hooks/useMediaGalleryHandlers.ts lines 67-146).
Downstream UI components consume similarly wide action/state structures, indicating diffuse seam contracts (src/shared/components/MediaGalleryItem/components/ShotActions.tsx lines 15-63).
findings.suggestion: Refactor gallery seams into narrower domain-oriented hooks (e.g., `useShotNavigationActions`, `useTaskDetailsActions`, `useMobileTapActions`) with explicit minimal inputs/outputs, then compose at the top-level container.

findings.summary: Dialog components expose inconsistent open-state contracts, reducing predictability of shared UI ownership patterns.
evidence: ConfirmDialog uses `open` + `onOpenChange` (src/shared/components/ConfirmDialog.tsx lines 35-40).
DescriptionModal uses `isOpen` + `onClose` but maps directly to `Dialog onOpenChange` (src/shared/components/LoraSelectorModal/components/DescriptionModal.tsx lines 6-14).
InstallInstructionsModal again uses `open` + `onOpenChange` (src/pages/Home/components/InstallInstructionsModal.tsx lines 7-43).
findings.suggestion: Standardize modal API contracts across shared and feature components (prefer one pattern, e.g., `open/onOpenChange`) and provide adapter wrappers only where required for legacy call sites.

findings.summary: Task creation internals rely on repeated hand-built payload bags, causing drift-prone low-level duplication.
evidence: `joinClips.ts` manually builds `orchestratorDetails` with many conditional assignments and nested override loops (`buildJoinClipsPayload`, lines ~292-409).

`individualTravelSegment.ts` repeats similar orchestration fields across both `buildOrchestratorDetails` and `buildTaskParamsPayload` (lines ~539-589 and ~606-658), including duplicated contract/context keys.

`travelBetweenImages/payloadBuilder.ts` constructs another large `orchestratorPayload` with many conditional spreads and defaults (lines ~119-217), overlapping responsibilities already present in sibling task builders.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared task-payload composition layer (base orchestration envelope + reusable optional-field helpers) and keep task-specific deltas small. Consolidate duplicated orchestration keys (`orchestration_contract`, parent/child linkage, run/orchestrator identifiers) into one typed builder used by all task modules.

findings.summary: Video lightbox rendering is split into multiple pass-through view-model layers with high indirection and limited leverage.
evidence: `VideoLightbox.tsx` builds mode/env/shared/edit/render models sequentially before rendering (`VideoLightboxContent`, lines ~95-131).

`useVideoLightboxRenderModel.tsx` creates several memoized pass-through objects (`lightboxCore`, `lightboxMedia`, `lightboxVariants`, `lightboxNavigation`) primarily reshaping existing values (lines ~180-257).

`useButtonGroupProps.ts` repeats the same shared fields into four separate builder functions, then wraps them in a `useMemo` keyed by whole `params` object (lines ~56-143), adding structure without reducing behavior complexity.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-through layers into a single typed render adapter per lightbox mode, and centralize shared button-context injection once. Keep only behavior-bearing transforms as separate hooks; inline pure property forwarding near the final consumer.

findings.summary: Inline edit state uses a legacy/new dual API with large field-by-field mapping, increasing maintenance cost and mismatch risk.
evidence: `useImageEditValue.ts` defines `UseImageEditValueLegacyParams` spanning six model interfaces, then remaps every field in `toModelsFromLegacy` (lines ~148-360).
`useInlineEditState.ts` already assembles `UseImageEditValueModels` via `buildImageEditValueModels` (lines ~252+), so the legacy conversion path duplicates the same shape translation responsibility.
`buildFormAndGenerationState` in `useImageEditValue.ts` contains duplicate assignment of `setLoraMode` (lines ~239-241), demonstrating drift/noise in manual mapping-heavy code.
findings.suggestion: Delete the legacy param shape and make `useImageEditValue` accept only `UseImageEditValueModels`. Generate model-to-state mapping via smaller grouped mappers (or typed pick helpers) to remove manual duplication and catch key drift at compile time.

findings.summary: Three near-identical timestamp hooks create micro-abstractions with minimal behavioral separation.
evidence: `useCompletedTimestamp` and `useProcessingTimestamp` both forward to `useLiveRelativeTimestamp` with only formatter and input-key differences (lines ~18-27 and ~18-27 respectively).
`useUpdatingTimestamp` follows the same wrapper pattern and adds another thin wrapper (`useTaskTimestamp`) for a slight formatter variant (lines ~26-58).
findings.suggestion: Replace these wrappers with one configurable timestamp hook (`useRelativeTimestamp({ dateInput, mode, abbreviate, includeDays })`) and keep optional task-specific formatter constants outside hooks.

findings.summary: Storage helpers expose migrated-away parameters that are no longer used, creating dishonest and noisy function internals.
evidence: `handleStorageOperations` accepts `_isServiceRole` but never references it.
`generateThumbnail` accepts `_sourceBytes`, `_userId`, and `_taskId` and ignores all of them.
Comments and naming imply richer behavior than actually executed, increasing cognitive load during maintenance.
findings.suggestion: Remove unused parameters from signatures and call sites, or reintroduce concrete behavior that uses them; avoid underscore-placeholder args unless there is an immediate compatibility bridge with a documented removal date.

findings.summary: Migration-related types are too permissive, allowing arbitrary keys and stale legacy fields to persist undetected.
evidence: `GenerationMetadata` ends with `[key: string]: unknown`, effectively allowing any extra metadata field.
`LegacyProjectImageSettings` extends `Record<string, unknown>` and is merged via `ProjectImageSettings & Partial<LegacyProjectImageSettings>`, widening accepted payloads during migration boundaries.
The sunset/removal comments are explicit, but type surfaces still make out-of-contract fields type-valid.
findings.suggestion: Replace open index signatures with a bounded `legacy` field or discriminated migration envelope, and narrow accepted keys as migrations complete so deprecated payloads become type errors instead of silently accepted data.

findings.summary: Thumbnail generation re-derives storage object paths instead of reusing the caller’s resolved path, creating avoidable internal branching and drift risk.
evidence: `handleStorageOperations` computes `objectPath` once, but `handleThumbnail` later rebuilds it from `(userId, taskId, filename)`.
The comment in `handleThumbnail` says object path is available from caller context, but the function signature does not take it directly.
Any future path policy change would require synchronized edits in multiple spots inside the same flow.
findings.suggestion: Pass resolved `objectPath` into `handleThumbnail` and remove local recomputation so path construction stays single-source and intention-revealing.

findings.summary: `useAutoTopup` is placed in `shared/hooks` despite owning billing/auth infrastructure behavior.
evidence: `useAutoTopup` imports `getSupabaseClient` from integrations, calls auth/session APIs, invokes `setup-auto-topup`, and invalidates credits query keys.
This behavior is product/billing-domain specific and not a generic reusable hook contract expected from `shared/hooks`.
The paired test is also under `shared/hooks/__tests__`, indicating the package is being used as a domain host rather than a true shared utility layer.
findings.suggestion: Move this hook (and its test) to a billing/credits domain package (for example `src/domains/billing/hooks`) and keep `shared` limited to UI-agnostic primitives/interfaces consumed by that domain.

findings.summary: `useBackgroundThumbnailGenerator` in `shared` owns generation-specific orchestration and cache-shape knowledge.
evidence: The hook manages a thumbnail generation queue, calls `generateAndUploadThumbnail`, and invalidates/patches `unifiedGenerationQueryKeys` data.
It directly mutates query data assuming an `items` collection, binding it tightly to one generation subsystem representation.
Placement in `shared/hooks` makes this domain ownership non-obvious and increases risk of cross-feature coupling.
findings.suggestion: Relocate the hook and tests to a generation/media domain folder and expose only a narrow interface (e.g., a service adapter) from shared utilities if needed.

findings.summary: Feature-specific generation UI components are organized as globally shared components.
evidence: `ActiveLoRAsDisplay` is tightly tied to LoRA model semantics (`ActiveLora`, trigger words, strength controls).
`AspectRatioSelector` depends on generation-oriented `ASPECT_RATIO_TO_RESOLUTION` and visualizer behavior.
Keeping such components under `shared/components` obscures ownership and invites unrelated features to couple to generation-specific UI contracts.
findings.suggestion: Move these components into domain/feature directories (for example `src/domains/generation/components`) and keep `shared/components` for cross-domain primitives only.

findings.summary: Root build config mixes generic tooling setup with product/deployment-specific policy.
evidence: `vite.config.ts` contains environment-specific preview host allowlists and manual vendor chunk strategy alongside baseline bundler setup.
`tailwind.config.ts` includes a large product-specific theme/palette surface in the same top-level config used for all app styling compilation.
This co-location increases top-level cognitive load and makes ownership between platform/config and product design policy less explicit.
findings.suggestion: Extract product/deployment policy into dedicated config modules (e.g., `config/vite/hosts.ts`, `config/theme/wesPalette.ts`) and keep root config files as thin composition entry points.

findings.summary: Core UI Button owns product-specific retro policy, blurring design-system ownership boundaries.
evidence: `button.tsx` imports and applies `retroButtonPolicy` and retro-specific variant/size type guards directly in the base Button implementation.
The shared primitive now has dual policy paths (core variants plus retro coercion fallback to `ghost`/`default`), so a generic UI contract is coupled to one stylistic subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Split retro behavior into a separate wrapper (e.g., `RetroButton`) or compose policy externally so `Button` remains style-system neutral and predictable.

findings.summary: Error handling module embeds global UI event protocol and reconfiguration rules that are only partially documented.
evidence: `handleError.ts` defines `ERROR_NOTIFICATION_EVENT`, dispatches browser `CustomEvent`s, and enforces one-notifier semantics by throwing on different re-registration.
`README.md` states notifier setup occurs at bootstrap, but does not specify strict single-notifier replacement behavior or event-channel coupling, leaving compatibility constraints implicit.
findings.suggestion: Promote notifier lifecycle/event semantics into an explicit contract doc (or typed public API) and separate core error normalization from UI transport wiring.

findings.summary: Supabase client entrypoint combines production ownership, instrumentation boot, and dev diagnostics in one contract surface.
evidence: `client.ts` performs instrumentation install, runtime initialization, singleton lifecycle, and optional debug global exposure from the same module.
`README.md` presents this file as canonical ownership; combining these concerns increases contract scope and makes import/use expectations less narrow than a pure client factory boundary.
findings.suggestion: Split into explicit layers (`clientSingleton`, `runtimeBootstrap`, `debugGlobals`) and keep `getSupabaseClient()` contract minimal to reduce surprise and compatibility drift.

findings.summary: Non-fatal catch blocks silently ignore errors instead of using a consistent reporting path.
evidence: `useAdjacentPagePreloader` catches prefetch failures with empty handlers (`catch {}` at lines 159-161 and 191), dropping context entirely.
`StrokeOverlay` also uses ignored catches for pointer capture/release (`catch { /* ok */ }` at lines 166 and 231), while other non-fatal UI errors in the same file call `handleError` (lines 384-387).
The codebase already has a standard non-fatal path (`handleError(..., { showToast: false })`), but these catches bypass it.
findings.suggestion: Standardize non-fatal catches to a shared helper (or direct `handleError`) with `showToast: false` and component-specific context so ignored operations are still observable and searchable.

findings.summary: Similar task/generation failures are sometimes rethrown and sometimes absorbed, creating unpredictable caller behavior.
evidence: `preloadGenerationTaskMappings` catches batch errors and only logs via `handleError` (lines 142-144), then continues without signaling partial failure.

Task creation modules (`imageGeneration`, `magicEdit`, `joinClips`) consistently `handleError` and then `throw` (imageGeneration.ts:428-430 and 513-514; magicEdit.ts:242-245 and 302-305; joinClips.ts:453-456).
These paths all operate in the same generation/task subsystem, but expose different failure contracts to callers.
findings.suggestion: Define one subsystem contract for these async helpers (e.g., always throw typed errors, or always return `{ ok, error }`), then align `generationTaskBridge` and task creators to that contract with explicit typing.

findings.summary: Discord sign-in handles errors locally but never propagates failure state, unlike adjacent mutation patterns.
evidence: `useDiscordSignIn` calls `handleError` on both returned auth errors and thrown exceptions (lines 26-31) but always resolves the callback with no error signal.
Mutation hooks in `useGenerationMutations` throw from mutation functions and let `onError` handle user messaging, preserving a usable failure signal for callers.
This causes inconsistent expectations for consumers of async user actions: some can branch on failure, while sign-in cannot.
findings.suggestion: Make `useDiscordSignIn` return a typed result (`{ ok: boolean; error?: AppError }`) or rethrow after `handleError`, and update callers to use the explicit contract.
